text: The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position. The surgeon enters the abdomen in the right upper quadrant using a 5mm optically guided trocar, preparing for the laparoscopic adrenalectomy.
The surgeon dissects and removes adhesions between the left lobe of the liver and the underlying omentum, carefully separating the two structures.
The surgeon encounters extensive adhesions between the adrenal tumor and the underlying capsule of the liver, indicating a challenging dissection area.
The laparoscopic view shows the adrenal tumor adjacent to the liver capsule, with no visible signs of gross invasion into the liver tissue.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection and bipolar electrocautery to carefully separate the adhesions between the adrenal tumor and the liver capsule.
The surgeon carefully avoids grasping the adrenal tumor directly, instead using blunt retraction on the liver to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon enters the retroperitoneal space, utilizing bipolar electrocautery to carefully dissect and separate tissue layers within this area.
The surgeon utilizes a curved bipolar electrocautery device for precise dissection and effective hemostasis, navigating around the adrenal tumor and liver capsule.
The surgeon carefully dissects to expose the blood supply to the adrenal tumor, revealing the adrenal vein and the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon has freed the adrenal mass from some of its attachments to the liver, revealing the size and shape of the mass within the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the adrenal mass from its inferior peritoneal attachments, carefully separating the tissue using blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon carefully avoids grasping the adrenal tumor directly, maintaining a clear view of the mass within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon grasps the pericapsular fat or a layer of peritoneum still attached to the capsule of the adrenal tumor, avoiding direct manipulation of the tumor itself.
The adrenal tumor is freed from its omental attachments and some remaining attachments to the adrenal gland, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The left lobe of the liver is visible hanging in the surgical field, providing exposure for the ongoing adrenalectomy procedure.
A self-retaining liver retractor is used to maintain the exposure of the surgical field, ensuring the left lobe of the liver remains elevated for the remainder of the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon focuses on the vascular supply to the adrenal mass, carefully identifying and preparing to manage the blood vessels associated with the tumor.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt dissection, suction, sharp dissection, and electrocautery to carefully separate the adrenal mass from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, where the large adrenal tumor presents challenges in maintaining adequate exposure, with the left lobe of the liver visible in the background.

The liver retractor is visible on the right, holding the liver away from the surgical field. The surgeon uses a blunt grasper to pull the adrenal tumor to the left, facilitating better exposure for dissection.
The surgeon dissects around the medial portion of the adrenal tumor, identifying several feeding vessels originating from the direction of the vena cava.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect and ligate the feeding vessels supplying the adrenal mass, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the inferior margin of the adrenal mass from the kidney, carefully delineating the boundary between the two structures.
The kidney is positioned directly adjacent to the adrenal tumor, highlighting the close anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon identifies a plane that is easily separable from the adrenal tumor, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon identifies a dominant vessel originating from the direction of the vena cava, suspected to be the adrenal vein, amidst the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and divides the dominant vessel, likely the adrenal vein, which was previously identified coming from the direction of the vena cava.
The surgeon prepares to take the adrenal vein using surgical clips, electrocautery, or a stapler, with the adrenal mass previously separated from the kidney.
The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to coagulate and divide the remaining vessels surrounding the adrenal gland.
The adrenal tumor is visible on the right side of the screen, with surrounding tissue and anatomical structures in view.
The surgeon divides the lateral peritoneal attachments, separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon transects the right triangular ligament and the superior attachments to the liver capsule, separating these structures during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the adrenal tumor, using previously mentioned techniques such as bipolar electrocautery and surgical clips.
The surgeon releases the adrenal tumor from its inferior and superior attachments, completing the dissection and freeing the tumor from surrounding tissues.
The adrenal tumor is fully mobilized and visible on the right side of the screen, having been released from all its attachments.
With the tumor fully mobilized and removed, the surgeon encounters a normal appearing adrenal gland, which is then excised.
The surgeon places the remaining adrenal gland into a 10mm endo-catch bag and the primary lesion into a 15mm endo bag, preparing them for removal from the surgical field.
